questions.stem: A patient is 6 hours post open abdominal surgery and reports pain rated 8/10. The patient is on scheduled ketorolac IV and a hydromorphone PCA but frequently presses the PCA demand button. Respiratory rate is 14 and SpO2 is 97%. Which is the most appropriate nursing response?

options.text: Contact the physician to increase the PCA demand dose — the patient clearly has inadequate pain control
Withhold all PCA doses and reassess in 2 hours — respiratory rate below 16 requires opioid restriction
Reassess pain using a validated scale, review PCA usage data and lockout history, maximize non-opioid adjuncts (IV acetaminophen if not given), and contact physician if pain remains uncontrolled
Administer naloxone 0.4 mg IV prophylactically to protect respiratory reserve before adjusting the PCA

questions.rationale: Effective post-operative pain management requires a systematic, multimodal approach. Steps: (1) Reassess pain thoroughly — PQRST (location, quality, timing — is this expected surgical pain or a new complication like wound dehiscence?); (2) Review PCA data — are demand attempts successful or being locked out? Is the patient pressing correctly? Is the demand dose reaching the lockout ceiling?; (3) Ensure non-opioid adjuncts are maximized: scheduled acetaminophen (reduces opioid requirements 20-30%), ketorolac, and consider regional analgesia; (4) Contact physician if pain remains uncontrolled despite optimized non-opioids. SpO2 97% + RR 14 indicates adequate ventilation — opioids are NOT contraindicated. The outdated practice of withholding opioids for RR <16 without clinical signs of respiratory depression is not evidence-based and results in undertreated pain. Prophylactic naloxone reverses analgesia and is inappropriate without signs of opioid toxicity.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "Respiratory rate is 14 and SpO2 is 97%" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Withhold all PCA doses and reassess in 2 hours — respiratory rate below 16 requires opioid restriction" moves care toward routine observation before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Administer naloxone 0.4 mg IV prophylactically to protect respiratory reserve before adjusting the PCA" moves care toward waiting for a callback before airway and breathing intervention.

questions.takeaway: Post-op pain: systematic multimodal assessment — maximize non-opioids (acetaminophen, NSAIDs), review PCA data, then escalate to MD. RR 14 + SpO2 97% with no signs of toxicity does NOT justify withholding opioids.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is preparing to administer insulin glargine (Lantus) and insulin aspart (NovoLog) to a patient with type 1 diabetes before breakfast. Which action by the nurse requires IMMEDIATE correction?

options.text: Drawing up each insulin in a separate syringe
Mixing glargine and aspart in the same syringe before administration
Injecting the aspart subcutaneously 15 minutes before the meal
Rotating injection sites between the abdomen and thigh

questions.rationale: Insulin glargine (Lantus) is a long-acting basal insulin with a specific pH formulation. It should NEVER be mixed with any other insulin, including rapid-acting insulins. Mixing glargine with other insulins alters its pH, causing it to crystallize and unpredictably changes both insulins' absorption profiles. The result is an unreliable pharmacokinetic profile that can cause severe hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia. Each insulin must be drawn in a separate syringe and administered as separate injections. Drawing up separately is correct. Injecting aspart before meals (pre-prandial) is appropriate. Site rotation is correct practice.

distractorRationales.a: "Drawing up each insulin in a separate syringe" misses the bedside risk because insulin glargine (Lantus) is a long-acting basal insulin with a specific pH formulation points to unstable glucose physiology. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave unstable glucose physiology untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Mixing glargine and aspart in the same syringe before administration" fits the safer response to the result is an unreliable pharmacokinetic profile that can cause severe hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia.
distractorRationales.d: "Rotating injection sites between the abdomen and thigh" misses the bedside risk because insulin glargine (Lantus) is a long-acting basal insulin with a specific pH formulation points to unstable glucose physiology. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.

questions.takeaway: Glargine (Lantus) = NEVER MIX with other insulins. It has its own pH and formulation. Mix-up can cause crystal formation and unpredictable glycemic effect. Use separate syringes, separate injections. Applies also to detemir (Levemir).

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for four patients on a medical-surgical unit. Which patient should the nurse assess first?

options.text: A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes who reports feeling 'shaky' and has a blood glucose of 62 mg/dL
A 44-year-old post-appendectomy (day 2) requesting pain medication, pain rated 5/10
A 71-year-old with heart failure who gained 1 lb overnight and is on daily weights
A 33-year-old with a UTI who asks when the next dose of antibiotics is due

questions.rationale: The patient with a blood glucose of 62 mg/dL has symptomatic hypoglycemia ('shaky'), which is an acute, life-threatening condition requiring immediate intervention. Untreated hypoglycemia can rapidly progress to seizures, loss of consciousness, and brain injury. The other patients represent important but lower-acuity concerns: the post-op patient has manageable pain, the heart failure patient's weight gain needs assessment but is not immediately life-threatening, and the UTI patient's antibiotic timing is a scheduled task.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses unstable glucose physiology; "A 44-year-old post-appendectomy (day 2) requesting pain medication, pain rated 5/10" moves care toward routine care before glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave unstable glucose physiology untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes who reports feeling 'shaky' and has a blood glucose of" fits the safer response to apply the ABCs and Maslow's hierarchy: acute metabolic emergencies (hypoglycemia, airway compromise) always outrank scheduled pain requests, routine.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses unstable glucose physiology; "A 33-year-old with a UTI who asks when the next dose of antibiotics is due" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization.

questions.takeaway: Apply the ABCs and Maslow's hierarchy: acute metabolic emergencies (hypoglycemia, airway compromise) always outrank scheduled pain requests, routine monitoring, and informational questions.

questions.stem: A community health nurse is teaching a group of nursing students about levels of prevention. Which example correctly illustrates primary prevention?

options.text: Providing mammography screening to detect early breast cancer in women over 40
Administering the influenza vaccine to a healthy adult before flu season
Prescribing physical therapy for a patient recovering from a hip fracture
Conducting a colonoscopy to identify and remove pre-cancerous polyps

questions.rationale: Primary prevention: actions taken before disease occurs to prevent onset. Examples: immunization, health education, smoking cessation counseling, fluoridated water, condom use. Secondary prevention: early detection of existing disease (screening) — mammography, colonoscopy, blood pressure screening, Pap smear. Tertiary prevention: reducing disability and restoring function in patients with established disease — rehabilitation, cardiac rehab, chronic disease management. Mammography and colonoscopy are secondary prevention (detecting disease before symptoms). Physical therapy post-fracture is tertiary prevention.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "A community health nurse is teaching a group of nursing students about levels of prevention" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "Prescribing physical therapy for a patient recovering from a hip fracture" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before circulation and perfusion support.
distractorRationales.d: "Conducting a colonoscopy to identify and remove pre-cancerous polyps" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Physical therapy post-fracture is tertiary prevention" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Primary prevention = before disease (vaccines, education); Secondary = early detection (screening); Tertiary = limit disability/restore function (rehab). Not to be confused with clinical levels of care.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse has four patients to assess. Which patient should the nurse see FIRST, applying Maslow's hierarchy of needs?

options.text: A patient who is anxious about an upcoming procedure
A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88%
A patient who needs discharge teaching before going home
A patient asking for pain medication rated 4/10

questions.rationale: Maslow's hierarchy places physiological needs at the foundation. Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority. A patient with RR 8 and SpO2 88% is in respiratory distress — an immediate life-threatening concern that takes absolute priority. Anxiety (A) is a safety/psychological need. Discharge teaching (C) is a self-actualization/cognitive need. Pain rated 4/10 (D) is physiological but not immediately life-threatening compared to respiratory failure.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "A patient who is anxious about an upcoming procedure" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "A patient who needs discharge teaching before going home" moves care toward ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88%" fits the safer response to pain rated 4/10 (D) is physiological but not immediately life-threatening compared to respiratory failure.

questions.takeaway: Maslow hierarchy on NCLEX: airway/breathing/circulation (physiological) always come before safety, love/belonging, esteem, and self-actualization.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is completing an admission assessment on a 79-year-old patient transferred from a nursing facility. Which combination of factors identifies the HIGHEST fall risk?

options.text: Age >65 and female gender
History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic drop >20 mmHg on standing)
Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29
Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion

questions.rationale: The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls in the elderly; (2) Polypharmacy with antihypertensives — alpha-blockers, diuretics, and calcium channel blockers commonly cause orthostatic hypotension and syncope; (3) Documented orthostatic hypotension — directly causes dizziness and falls upon position change. Both the Morse Fall Scale and STRATIFY risk tool weight prior falls most heavily. Key interventions: bed in lowest position, non-skid footwear, call light within reach, scheduled toileting (most falls occur during nighttime toileting), orthostatic vital signs before ambulation, and urgent medication review. Age and gender alone are poor predictors without functional factors.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses medication toxicity; "Age >65 and female gender" moves care toward routine care before medication-safety intervention.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave medication toxicity untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic" fits the safer response to highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward false reassurance instead of medication-safety intervention. The stem cue "Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension" makes medication toxicity the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. Prior fall is the strongest single predictor. Target modifiable risk factors aggressively.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: An RN is caring for four patients and needs to delegate tasks. Which task is appropriate to assign to an unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP/CNA)?

options.text: Assess the breath sounds of a patient with new-onset dyspnea
Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube
Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery
Teach a diabetic patient how to perform foot care

questions.rationale: UAPs can perform tasks that are routine, repetitive, and do not require nursing judgment or assessment. Ambulating a stable postoperative patient 24 hours post-hip replacement is a safe, predictable task for UAP after the RN has assessed the patient's stability. Assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated. Administering tube feedings (B) — in many states, enteral feeding via established G-tube can be delegated to trained UAPs, but the skill level required is greater than ambulation; this varies by state — ambulation is universally appropriate for CNA delegation. Patient education (D) is an RN responsibility requiring professional judgment.

distractorRationales.a: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. "Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery" fits the safer response to uAPs can perform routine, non-assessment tasks: ambulation, ADLs, vital signs on stable patients, I&O — assessment, teaching.
distractorRationales.b: "Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube" misses the bedside risk because assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Teach a diabetic patient how to perform foot care" moves care toward teaching or reassurance before airway and breathing intervention.

questions.takeaway: UAPs can perform routine, non-assessment tasks: ambulation, ADLs, vital signs on stable patients, I&O — assessment, teaching, and medication administration remain with the RN.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient delivers vaginally and 30 minutes later has an estimated blood loss of 750 mL with a soft, boggy uterus displaced to the right. BP is 94/58, HR 118. Which sequence of interventions is correct?

options.text: IV oxytocin infusion, bimanual uterine compression, empty the bladder, call the provider
Empty the bladder, fundal massage, IV oxytocin infusion, call the provider if no improvement
Call the provider, obtain type and crossmatch, begin blood transfusion
Place in Trendelenburg, administer IV epinephrine, call for hysterectomy consent

questions.rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) from uterine atony management: (1) Massage the fundus — stimulates uterine contraction. (2) Empty the bladder — a full bladder displaces the uterus and prevents contraction (uterus displaced to the right = full bladder). (3) IV oxytocin — first-line uterotonic. (4) Notify provider if no response. Bladder emptying is critical and often overlooked — the displaced, boggy uterus signals bladder distension. Epinephrine (D) and hysterectomy are not indicated at this stage.

distractorRationales.a: "IV oxytocin infusion, bimanual uterine compression, empty the bladder, call the provider" misses the bedside risk because bP is 94/58, HR 118 points to bleeding or anticoagulation injury. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.
distractorRationales.c: "Call the provider, obtain type and crossmatch, begin blood transfusion" relies on notification without the bedside safety step. The cue "BP is 94/58, HR 118" raises concern for bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so bleeding-risk intervention is needed before waiting for a callback.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward incomplete escalation instead of bleeding-risk intervention. The stem cue "(3) IV oxytocin — first-line uterotonic" makes bleeding or anticoagulation injury the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Boggy uterus displaced to the right after delivery = full bladder; empty bladder first, then fundal massage + oxytocin.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A post-operative patient on day 2 following abdominal surgery complains of left calf pain, warmth, and swelling. Vital signs are stable. Which action is the NURSE'S PRIORITY?

options.text: Elevate the leg and apply warm compresses
Perform Homan's sign assessment and document the result
Notify the provider and anticipate orders for duplex ultrasound
Administer the scheduled enoxaparin and continue monitoring

questions.rationale: Unilateral calf pain, warmth, and swelling in a post-operative patient are classic signs of deep vein thrombosis (DVT). The nurse's priority is to notify the provider so imaging (duplex ultrasound — gold standard for DVT) can be ordered promptly. Homan's sign (B) is unreliable and has been removed from clinical guidelines. Warmth and elevation (A) delay diagnosis. Administering the next prophylactic dose (D) without a confirmed DVT diagnosis and provider notification is inappropriate.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "Elevate the leg and apply warm compresses" moves care toward routine care before circulation and perfusion support.
distractorRationales.b: "Perform Homan's sign assessment and document the result" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Administering the next prophylactic dose (D) without a confirmed DVT diagnosis and provider notification is inappropriate" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: Because the nurse's priority is to notify the provider so imaging (duplex ultrasound — gold standard for DVT) can suggests circulation or perfusion, "Administer the scheduled enoxaparin and continue monitoring" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.

questions.takeaway: Post-op calf pain + swelling = suspect DVT; notify provider and anticipate duplex ultrasound — Homan's sign is unreliable.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A client with depression says: 'I don't see the point in trying anymore. Nothing I do matters.' Which nurse response is most therapeutic?

options.text: 'I understand. Sometimes things feel hopeless, but you have so much to live for.'
'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.'
'Everyone feels that way sometimes. It will get better soon.'
'You shouldn't think like that. Let's focus on the positive things in your life.'

questions.rationale: Option B is an open-ended exploration that invites the client to elaborate, demonstrating active listening without judgment. It also screens for suicidal ideation — 'nothing I do matters' and 'no point in trying' may signal passive suicidal thinking. Option A uses false reassurance ('so much to live for') and assumes understanding without exploring further. Option C minimizes the client's experience ('everyone feels that way'). Option D is non-therapeutic confrontation that dismisses the client's feelings.

distractorRationales.a: "'I understand. Sometimes things feel hopeless, but you have so much to live for.'" misses the bedside risk because therapeutic communication safety points to clinical safety risk. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: "'Everyone feels that way sometimes. It will get better soon.'" misses the bedside risk because therapeutic communication safety points to clinical safety risk. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave clinical safety risk untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.'" fits the safer response to therapeutic communication safety.

questions.takeaway: Therapeutic communication: use open-ended statements to explore meaning, especially when statements may signal suicidal ideation.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.
